summary: Anonymous 14th-century Middle English poet, also known as the Gawain Poet. All four attributed poems (Pearl, Sir Gawain and the Green Knight, Cleanness, and Patience) are found in the Cotton Nero A.x manuscript. Part of the Alliterative Revival. Known for using the 'bob and wheel' metrical device. Also sometimes credited with a poem about St. Erkenwald.

works.name: Sir Gawain and the Green Knight
works.description: A 14th-century Arthurian romance in alliterative verse, using a 'bob and wheel' metrical device (a pair of stressed syllables followed by a rhyming four-line stanza). Sir Gawain beheads the Green Knight and accepts a challenge to be beheaded himself in a year and a day. Lady Bertilak tests Gawain by pretending to seduce him. Gawain turns down her offer of a ring but accepts a silken girdle/sash/belt that supposedly grants invulnerability. He breaks the rules of a game by keeping the girdle rather than trading it (animal carcasses are traded for kisses). He receives a nick on the neck rather than full beheading, as punishment. The confrontation was planned by Morgan le Fay. Lord Bertilak is the Green Knight. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Gawain accepts a girdle/sash/belt that supposedly grants invulnerability; breaks the rules by keeping it; receives a nick on the neck instead of beheading

works.name: Pearl
works.description: An allegorical poem about a father grieving the loss of his daughter, who is named for the title object (a pearl). The narrator falls asleep in a 'garden green' on a mound covered in herbs and dreams of seeing a maiden (his daughter) across a river representing death, who lies out of reach. The maiden teaches him about sin and grace and shows him the Heavenly Jerusalem / New Jerusalem. She is part of a procession of virgins in heaven. The first five stanzas end with the phrase 'without a spot.' Wikipedia

clues.clue: A father grieves his lost daughter; dreams of seeing a maiden across a river/stream representing death; learns about the New Jerusalem
examples: a father dreams of crossing a stream to a beautiful maiden
the narrator learns about the New Jerusalem from a maiden he identifies with the title lost object
a man dreams that he sees a woman across a stream, who teaches him about sin and grace before showing him the Heavenly Jerusalem

clues.clue: The first five stanzas end with 'without a spot'; the narrator falls asleep in a 'garden green' on a mound covered in herbs
examples: The first five stanzas of a poem by this author all end with the phrase 'without a spot' to describe an object that fell down a mound covered in herbs.

clues.clue: The maiden/daughter is part of a procession of virgins in heaven
examples: a man is told that his daughter is part of a procession of virgins in heaven

works.name: Cleanness (Purity)
works.description: An alliterative poem urging mankind to follow the title virtue of cleanness/purity. Uses episodes of Noah's ark and the Flood, Abraham and Lot, and the writing on the wall at Belshazzar's Feast. The description of the Dead Sea and vessels at Belshazzar's Feast are drawn from Mandeville's Travels. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Uses images of Belshazzar's Feast and the Flood to illustrate the title concept of purity/cleanness
examples: One of this author's poems uses images of Belshazzar's Feast and the Flood to illustrate its title concept

clues.clue: Dead Sea and vessels at Belshazzar's Feast drawn from Mandeville's Travels
examples: This poem's description of the Dead Sea and the vessels used at Belshazzar's Feast are mainly drawn from Mandeville's Travels.

works.name: Patience
works.description: An alliterative poem that identifies the title virtue with poverty and describes its role in the story of Jonah. Mocks Jonah for not possessing the title quality, leading to his being swallowed by a whale. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Identifies the title virtue with poverty; describes the story of Jonah; mocks Jonah for not possessing patience, leading to being swallowed by a whale
examples: This author wrote a poem identifying the title virtue with poverty and describing its role in the story of Jonah.
This poem from the manuscript mocks Jonah for not possessing the title quality, leading to his being swallowed by a whale.

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: The Pearl Poet is an anonymous 14th-century Middle English author. All four attributed poems are found in the Cotton Nero A.x manuscript. The poet is grouped with the Alliterative Revival alongside Langland's Piers Plowman. Also sometimes credited with a 352-line alliterative poem about St. Erkenwald, the late-seventh-century bishop of London. A saint sheds a tear on a reanimated corpse which then dissolves into dust in one attributed poem.

comprehensive_summary:
The Pearl Poet (also called the Gawain Poet) is an anonymous 14th-century Middle English author. All four of the attributed poems — Pearl, Sir Gawain and the Green Knight, Cleanness (or Purity), and Patience — are found in the Cotton Nero A.x manuscript. The poet is grouped with the Alliterative Revival, alongside William Langland's Piers Plowman.

Sir Gawain and the Green Knight is an Arthurian romance in alliterative verse that uses a 'bob and wheel' metrical device — a short half-line of two stressed syllables followed by a rhyming four-line stanza. Sir Gawain beheads the Green Knight and accepts a challenge to be beheaded in return in a year and a day. Lady Bertilak tests Gawain by pretending to seduce him. Gawain turns down her offer of a ring but accepts a silken girdle (green belt/sash) that supposedly grants invulnerability. Under the rules of a game, animal carcasses are to be traded for kisses, but Gawain breaks the rules by secretly keeping the girdle. He receives only a nick on the neck rather than a full beheading. The confrontation was planned by Morgan le Fay. Lord Bertilak is revealed to be the Green Knight.

Pearl is an allegorical poem about a father grieving his lost daughter, identified with the title object (a pearl). After falling asleep in a 'garden green' on a mound covered in herbs, the narrator dreams of a different garden where his daughter appears as a maiden across a river representing death. The first five stanzas end with the phrase 'without a spot.' The maiden teaches him about sin and grace and shows him the Heavenly Jerusalem / New Jerusalem. She is part of a procession of virgins in heaven.

Cleanness (or Purity) is an alliterative poem urging mankind to follow the title virtue, using episodes of Noah's ark and the Flood, Abraham and Lot, and the writing on the wall at Belshazzar's Feast. The description of the Dead Sea and vessels at Belshazzar's Feast are drawn from Mandeville's Travels.

Patience identifies the title virtue with poverty and describes its role in the story of Jonah, mocking Jonah for not possessing patience, which leads to his being swallowed by a whale.

The Pearl Poet is also sometimes credited with a 352-line alliterative poem about St. Erkenwald, the late-seventh-century bishop of London. In one attributed poem, a saint sheds a tear on a reanimated corpse which then dissolves into dust.
